cvcqv 1.0.3
  • Reference
  • Articles
    • Coefficient of Quartile Variation: cqv_versatile
    • Coefficient of Variation: cv_versatile
  • Changelog

Coefficient of Variation: cv_versatile

Maani Beigy

February 18, 2019

Source: vignettes/cv_versatile.Rmd
cv_versatile.Rmd

Coefficient of Variation

Coefficient of variation (CVCV) is a measure of relative dispersion representing the degree of variability relative to the mean (Albatineh, Kibria, Wilcox, & Zogheib, 2014). Since cv is unitless, it is useful for comparison of variables with different units (Albatineh et al., 2014). It is also a measure of homogeneity. The population coefficient of variation is:
CV=σμ,CV = \frac{\sigma}{\mu}, where σ\sigma is the population standard deviation and μ\mu is the population mean. Almost always, we analyze data from samples but want to generalize it as the population’s parameter (Albatineh et al., 2014). Its sample’s estimate is given as:
cv=sdX‾cv = \frac{sd}{\bar{X}}
where sdsd is the sample standard deviation, the square root of the unbiased estimator of population variance, and X‾\bar{X} is the sample mean. The corrected cv to account for the sample size is: cvcorr=cv*(1−14(n−1)+1ncv2+12(n−1)2) cv_{corr} = cv * \biggl(1 - \frac{1}{4(n-1)} + \frac{1}{n}cv^2 + \frac{1}{2 (n-1)^2} \biggr) There are various methods for the calculation of confidence intervals (CI) for cv. All of them are fruitful and have particular use cases. Some of them are model-based hence their usage depends the assumptions regarding the distribution of data. For sake of versatility, we cover almost all of these methods in cvcqv package. Here, we explain them along with some examples:

Kelley Confidence Interval

Let us assume that CV follows a noncentral t distribution, when the parent population of the scores is normally-distributed, with noncentrality (λ\lambda) parameter:
λ=ncv \lambda = \frac{\sqrt{n}}{cv} with v degrees of freedom, where v=n−1v = n - 1. Let 1−α1 - \alpha be the CI coverage with αL+αU=α\alpha_L + \alpha_U = \alpha in which αL\alpha_L is the the proportion of times that cv will be less than the lower confidence bound and αU\alpha_U the proportion of times that cv will be greater than the upper confidence bound in the CI procedure (Kelley, 2007). The lower confidence tile for λ\lambda is is the noncentrality parameter that results in t(1−αL,v,λL)=λ̂t_{(1-\alpha_L,v,\lambda_L)}=\hat{\lambda} and the upper confidence tile for λ\lambda is is the noncentrality parameter that results in t(αU,v,λU)=λ̂t_{(\alpha_U,v,\lambda_U)}=\hat{\lambda}, where t(1−αL,v,λL)=λ̂t_{(1-\alpha_L,v,\lambda_L)}=\hat{\lambda} is the value of noncentral t distribution at the 1−αL1-\alpha_Lquantile with noncentrality parameter λL\lambda_L and t(αU,v,λU)=λ̂t_{(\alpha_U,v,\lambda_U)}=\hat{\lambda} is the value of noncentral t distribution at the αU\alpha_Uquantile with noncentrality parameter λU\lambda_U, respectively (Kelley, 2007).
Afterwards, we transform the tiles of the confidence interval for λ\lambda, by dividing the tiles by n\sqrt{n} and thereafter inverting them; the CI limits of cvcv will be obtained:
p[(λUn)−1≤CV≤(λLn)−1]=1−α p\left[\biggl(\frac{\lambda_U}{\sqrt{n}}\biggr)^{-1} \le CV \le \biggl(\frac{\lambda_L}{\sqrt{n}}\biggr)^{-1}\right] = 1-\alpha where pp stands for probability. Thanks to package MBESS (Kelley, 2018) for the computation of confidence limits for the noncentrality parameter from a t distribution (conf.limits.nct), cvcv will be obtained as:

x <- c(
    0.2, 0.5, 1.1, 1.4, 1.8, 2.3, 2.5, 2.7, 3.5, 4.4,
    4.6, 5.4, 5.4, 5.7, 5.8, 5.9, 6.0, 6.6, 7.1, 7.9
)
cv_versatile(
    x, 
    na.rm = TRUE, 
    digits = 3, 
    method = "kelley", 
    correction = TRUE, 
    alpha = 0.05
)
## $method
## [1] "Corrected cv with Kelley 95% CI"
## 
## $statistics
##      est  lower upper
##   58.058 41.467 98.51

McKay Confidence Interval

McKay (McKay, 1932) introduced the following CI for cvcv; considering u1=χv,1−α/22u_1 = \chi_{v,1-\alpha/2}^2 and u1=χv,α/22u_1 = \chi_{v,\alpha/2}^2 being the 100(1−α/2)%100(1-\alpha/2)\% and 100(α/2)%100(\alpha/2)\% percentile of the χ2\chi^2 distribution with v=n−1v = n-1 degrees of freedom, respectively (Albatineh et al., 2014):
(cv[(u1v−1)(cv)2+u1v]−1/2≤CV≤cv[(u2v−1)(cv)2+u2v]−1/2) \biggl(cv\left[\biggl(\frac{u_1}{v}-1\biggr)(cv)^{2}+\frac{u_1}{v}\right]^{-1/2} \le CV \le cv \left[\biggl(\frac{u_2}{v}-1\biggr)(cv)^{2}+\frac{u_2}{v}\right]^{-1/2}\biggr) Let us calculate the 95% CI for our variable xx according to McKay’s method (McKay, 1932):

cv_versatile(
    x, 
    na.rm = TRUE, 
    digits = 3, 
    method = "mckay", 
    correction = TRUE, 
    alpha = 0.05
)
## $method
## [1] "Corrected cv with McKay 95% CI"
## 
## $statistics
##      est  lower   upper
##   58.058 41.622 109.367

Miller Confidence Interval

Miller (Edward Miller, 1991) introduced the following CI for cvcv; considering Zα/2Z_{\alpha/2} being the (1−α/2)(1-\alpha/2) percentile of the standard normal distribution (Albatineh et al., 2014): (cv−Zα/2(cv2v)(12+cv2)≤CV≤cv+Zα/2(cv2v)(12+cv2)) \biggl(cv - Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{ \biggl(\frac{cv^2}{v}\biggr)\biggl(\frac{1}{2}+cv^2\biggr)} \le CV \le cv + Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{ \biggl(\frac{cv^2}{v}\biggr)\biggl(\frac{1}{2}+cv^2\biggr)} \biggr) where v=n−1v = n-1 is the degree of freedom.
Let us calculate the 95% CI for xx according to Miller’s method (Edward Miller, 1991):

cv_versatile(
    x, 
    na.rm = TRUE, 
    digits = 3, 
    method = "miller", 
    correction = TRUE, 
    alpha = 0.05
)
## $method
## [1] "Corrected cv with Miller 95% CI"
## 
## $statistics
##      est  lower  upper
##   58.058 34.173 81.942

Vangel Confidence Interval

Vangel (Vangel, 1996) proposed the following CI for cvcv; which is a modification on McKay’s CI: (cv[(u1+1v−1)(cv)2+u1v]−1/2≤CV≤cv[(u2+1v−1)(cv)2+u2v]−1/2) \biggl(cv\left[\biggl(\frac{u_1+1}{v}-1\biggr)(cv)^{2}+\frac{u_1}{v}\right]^{-1/2} \le CV \le cv \left[\biggl(\frac{u_2+1}{v}-1\biggr)(cv)^{2}+\frac{u_2}{v}\right]^{-1/2}\biggr) Let us calculate the 95% CI for xx according to Vangel’s method (Vangel, 1996):

cv_versatile(
    x, 
    na.rm = TRUE, 
    digits = 3, 
    method = "vangel", 
    correction = TRUE, 
    alpha = 0.05
)
## $method
## [1] "Corrected cv with Vangel 95% CI"
## 
## $statistics
##      est  lower   upper
##   58.058 41.443 106.237

Mahmoudvand-Hassani Confidence Interval

Mahmoudvand and Hassani (Mahmoudvand & Hassani, 2009) proposed the following CI for cvcv; which is obtained using ranked set sampling (RSS):
(cv2−Cn+Z1−α/21−Cn2≤CV≤cv2−Cn−Z1−α/21−Cn2) \biggl(\frac{cv}{2-C_n+Z_{1-\alpha/2}\sqrt{1-C_n^2}} \le CV \le \frac{cv}{2-C_n-Z_{1-\alpha/2}\sqrt{1-C_n^2}} \biggr) where Cn=2n−1Γ(n/2)Γ((n−1)/2),Γ(n)=(n−1)! C_n=\sqrt{\frac{2}{n-1}}\frac{\Gamma{(n/2)}}{\Gamma{((n-1)/2)}}, \Gamma(n)=(n-1)! Let us now calculate the 95% CI for xx according to Mahmoudvand-Hassani’s method (Mahmoudvand & Hassani, 2009):

cv_versatile(
    x, 
    na.rm = TRUE, 
    digits = 3, 
    method = "mahmoudvand_hassani", 
    correction = TRUE, 
    alpha = 0.05
)
## $method
## [1] "Corrected cv with Mahmoudvand-Hassani 95% CI"
## 
## $statistics
##      est lower  upper
##   58.058 43.69 83.264

Normal Approximation Confidence Interval

Wararit Panichkitkosolkul (Panichkitkosolkul, 2013) proposed the following CI for cvcv; which is a normal approximation: (cvCn+1+Z1−α/21−Cn+12≤CV≤cvCn+1−Z1−α/21−Cn+12) \biggl(\frac{cv}{C_{n+1}+Z_{1-\alpha/2}\sqrt{1-C_{n+1}^2}} \le CV \le \frac{cv}{C_{n+1}-Z_{1-\alpha/2}\sqrt{1-C_{n+1}^2}} \biggr) where Cn+1=1−(1/2n)C_{n+1}=\sqrt{1-(1/2n)}
Now we calculate the normal approximation 95% CI for xx according to Panichkitkosolkul (Panichkitkosolkul, 2013):

cv_versatile(
    x, 
    na.rm = TRUE, 
    digits = 3, 
    method = "normal_approximation", 
    correction = TRUE, 
    alpha = 0.05
)
## $method
## [1] "Corrected cv with Normal Approximation 95% CI"
## 
## $statistics
##      est  lower  upper
##   58.058 44.752 85.691

Shortest-Length Confidence Interval

Panichkitkosolkul (Panichkitkosolkul, 2013) has also introduced the following CI for cvcv:
(cvvb≤CV≤cvva) \biggl(\frac{cv\sqrt{v}}{\sqrt{b}} \le CV \le \frac{cv\sqrt{v}}{\sqrt{a}} \biggr) with v=n−1v = n-1 degrees of freedom. Then, shortest-length 95% CI for xx is:

cv_versatile(
    x, 
    na.rm = TRUE, 
    digits = 3, 
    method = "shortest_length", 
    correction = TRUE, 
    alpha = 0.05
)
## $method
## [1] "Corrected cv with Shortest-Length 95% CI"
## 
## $statistics
##      est  lower  upper
##   58.058 42.221 81.411

Equal-Tailed Confidence Interval

The 100(1−α)%100(1-\alpha)\% equal-tailed CI for cvcv can be obtained as: (cvvχv,1−α/22≤CV≤cvvχv,α/22) \biggl(\frac{cv\sqrt{v}}{\sqrt{\chi_{v,1-\alpha/2}^2}} \le CV \le \frac{cv\sqrt{v}}{\sqrt{\chi_{v,\alpha/2}^2}} \biggr) where χv,α/22\chi_{v,\alpha/2}^2 and χv,1−α/22\chi_{v,1-\alpha/2}^2 are the 100(α/2)100(\alpha/2) and 100(1−α/2)100(1-\alpha/2) percentiles of the central χ2\chi^2 distribution with vv degrees of freedom, respectively (Panichkitkosolkul, 2013).
Then, equal-tailed 95% CI for xx is:

cv_versatile(
    x, 
    na.rm = TRUE, 
    digits = 3, 
    method = "equal_tailed", 
    correction = TRUE, 
    alpha = 0.05
)
## $method
## [1] "Corrected cv with Equal-Tailed 95% CI"
## 
## $statistics
##      est  lower  upper
##   58.058 44.152 84.797

Bootstrap Confidence Intervals

Thanks to package boot (Canty & Ripley, 2017) we can obtain bootstrap CI around cvcv:

cv_versatile(
    x, 
    na.rm = TRUE, 
    digits = 3, 
    method = "basic", 
    correction = TRUE, 
    alpha = 0.05
)
## $method
## [1] "Corrected cv with Basic Bootstrap 95% CI"
## 
## $statistics
##      est  lower  upper
##   58.058 36.188 78.903

All Available Methods

In conclusion, we can observe CIs calculated by all available methods:

cv_versatile(
    x, 
    na.rm = TRUE, 
    digits = 3, 
    method = "all", 
    correction = FALSE, 
    alpha = 0.05
)
## $method
## [1] "All methods"
## 
## $statistics
##                         est  lower   upper
## kelley               57.774 41.467  98.510
## mckay                57.774 41.441 108.482
## miller               57.774 34.053  81.494
## vangel               57.774 41.264 105.424
## mahmoudvand_hassani  57.774 43.476  82.857
## equal_tailed         57.774 43.936  84.382
## shortest_length      57.774 42.014  81.012
## normal_approximation 57.774 44.533  85.272
## norm                 57.774 39.102  77.887
## basic                57.774 37.531  77.603
##                                                        description
## kelley                                       cv with Kelley 95% CI
## mckay                                         cv with McKay 95% CI
## miller                                       cv with Miller 95% CI
## vangel                                       cv with Vangel 95% CI
## mahmoudvand_hassani             cv with Mahmoudvand-Hassani 95% CI
## equal_tailed                           cv with Equal-Tailed 95% CI
## shortest_length                     cv with Shortest-Length 95% CI
## normal_approximation           cv with Normal Approximation 95% CI
## norm                 cv with Normal Approximation Bootstrap 95% CI
## basic                               cv with Basic Bootstrap 95% CI

References

Albatineh, A. N., Kibria, B. M. G., Wilcox, M. L., & Zogheib, B. (2014). Confidence interval estimation for the population coefficient of variation using ranked set sampling: A simulation study. Journal of Applied Statistics, 41(4), 733–751. https://doi.org/10.1080/02664763.2013.847405
Canty, A., & Ripley, B. (2017). boot: Bootstrap R (S-Plus) Functions. R package version 1.3-20.
Edward Miller, G. (1991). Asymptotic test statistics for coefficients of variation. Communications in Statistics-Theory and Methods, 20(10), 3351–3363.
Kelley, K. (2007). Sample size planning for the coefficient of variation from the accuracy in parameter estimation approach. Behavior Research Methods, 39(4), 755–766. https://doi.org/10.3758/BF03192966
Kelley, K. (2018). MBESS: The MBESS R Package. R package version 4.4. 3. Retrieved from https://cran.r-project.org/package=MBESS
Mahmoudvand, R., & Hassani, H. (2009). Two new confidence intervals for the coefficient of variation in a normal distribution. Journal of Applied Statistics, 36(4), 429–442.
McKay, A. T. (1932). Distribution of the Coefficient of Variation and the Extended" t" Distribution. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society, 95(4), 695–698.
Panichkitkosolkul, W. (2013). Confidence Intervals for the Coefficient of Variation in a Normal Distribution with a Known Population Mean. Journal of Probability and Statistics, 2013, 1–11. https://doi.org/10.1155/2013/324940
Vangel, M. G. (1996). Confidence intervals for a normal coefficient of variation. The American Statistician, 50(1), 21–26.

Developed by Maani Beigy.
